headline: Agreed text, warning shots: the Hormuz deal is 14 points long and unsigned
deck: Washington and Tehran say they've agreed on the wording of a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. In the same 72 hours: US airstrikes, Iranian warning shots at a tanker, and a navy escorting oil out of the Gulf. There's a document. Nobody has signed it.
body: The deal is fourteen points long, and it doesn't exist yet. Iran's foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, went on state television and described it almost fondly: a "fourteen-point memorandum of understanding" that "has not yet been signed, and until the very last moment things may still shift." [E3] That last clause is the whole story. There's a text. Both sides like the words. Nobody has signed it.

Officially, the hard part is over. A US official said the two countries had agreed on the wording and could sign "in coming days" — then added they were "not quite at the finish line yet." [E1] Pakistan's prime minister, who's been carrying messages between the capitals, said the text was final, with the nuclear questions pushed into a second round lasting about sixty days. [E2] Markets spent the week pricing the signature. The record only supports the draft.

Here's the part the word "deal" is covering for: it's being negotiated over gunfire. The strait the memo would reopen is the most important oil chokepoint on earth — about 20 million barrels a day, a fifth of the world's, used to pass through it. [E4] It isn't flowing normally now. The US energy secretary says the American military is helping move 7 million barrels a day out of the Gulf, about half of what's stuck. [E5] A navy escorting half the normal flow is what "reopening" means this week.

And nobody paused the coercion for the diplomacy. The EU, using a new freedom-of-navigation sanctions power for the first time, said Iran's Revolutionary Guard had "assumed control of the Strait of Hormuz," set up a toll, and was checking ships' papers before letting them through. [E6] On the night of 12 June, Iranian state media reported warning fire at a vessel near Sirik — the same hours the signing was supposedly days away.

So the paper prints the gap, not the headline. There's a fourteen-point text, a foreign minister narrating it on TV, and a strait that a foreign navy is running by force while a second navy escorts tankers through it. The deal is real. So are the warning shots. It isn't a settlement until it's signed — and it's being signed inside a shooting gallery.

art.caption: IRGC-administered narrows (red) · guarded tanker transit lane (amber) · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1
art.title: Strait of Hormuz

dissent.argument: It's the timing, not the facts. 'Days away' is the most-broken promise in US–Iran diplomacy — the 2015 endgame and the 2021–22 Vienna rounds both ran 'days away' for weeks, and one of them collapsed. The hardest pieces here are parked in a sixty-day second round. Israel isn't even at the table, and won't pull back. And the foreign minister himself says the text might still move. A draft read out on TV is a bargaining chip, not a signature.

evidence_box.fragment: US and Iran signal an initial peace deal is near; a US official says signing is possible in coming days but they are "not quite at the finish line yet."
Pakistan's prime minister says the US and Iran have agreed on the wording of a deal; nuclear-technical details to be worked out over roughly 60 days.

evidence_box.source: Araghchi, Iranian state TV — monitored via X (Klaxon sensor net)
evidence_box.fragment: "a fourteen-point memorandum of understanding… it has not yet been signed, and until the very last moment things may still shift."

source_note.provenance_note: Iranian state TV remarks, captured via X monitoring; no stable public-URL artifact — carried as social-grade, corroborated by the agreed-text wire reporting [E1][E2].

evidence_box.fragment: About 20 million barrels per day of oil and condensate transited Hormuz in 2024, roughly 20% of global petroleum-liquids consumption.
US Energy Secretary Chris Wright says the US military is helping move 7 million barrels a day out of the Persian Gulf — about half the flow that had been stuck.

The IRGC Navy "assumed control of the Strait of Hormuz," "implemented a toll system," and required vessels to provide papers before being "allowed to transit"; first use of the EU freedom-of-navigation sanctions regime.
